nativeRange.habitat: Low-altitude, heavily vegetated rainforest streams with slow to moderate current, clear water, and abundant aquatic plants; found in the Musa and Kwagila river basins of the Northern and Milne Bay provinces

decorPreferences: Dense planting along sides and back for cover and refuge
Fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg-laying
Floating plants for surface cover and diffused light
Gentle filtration — sponge filter or low-flow outlet strongly preferred

diet.notes: A micro-carnivore with a small mouth. Generally does well on live and frozen micro-foods: freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, daphnia, moina, cyclops, micro worms, and vinegar eels. Will typically accept high-quality micro-pellets and nano flakes but tends to show better condition on live or frozen fare. Small portions fed multiple times daily tend to work better than a single large meal.

avoidWith: Larger or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding

summary: A tiny, dazzling rainbowfish from PNG's forest streams, with males sporting a distinctively forked yellow-edged caudal fin. Best kept in a planted school of 6 or more; pairs well with shrimp and micro-rasboras.
careNotes:
The Forktail Rainbowfish (also sold as Forktail Blue-Eye) is a member of Pseudomugilidae, the blue-eye family — sister family to Melanotaeniidae. Males tend to display more vivid coloration and conspicuously forked, yellow-edged caudal fins; females are typically smaller and plainer. The sexual dimorphism is one of the key visual draws of the species in a well-kept planted tank.

All aquarium specimens are generally considered to derive from captive-bred lines; wild-caught fish have not been commercially available for decades. The species tends to be sensitive to water quality fluctuations, particularly in biologically immature tanks, so a fully cycled aquarium is generally advised before introduction.

Group size matters: a school of at least 6 (ideally 8–10) tends to produce natural shoaling behavior and active male display. Smaller groups often produce shy, unimpressive fish. Males display to each other by flaring fins — non-violent competition that can be a highlight of the tank. A slightly female-biased sex ratio (e.g., 2 males : 4 females) is commonly recommended to distribute male attention.

Gentlest possible water flow is strongly advised; this species comes from slow, clear forest streams and has been reported to struggle in turbulent or high-flow aquariums. A sponge filter or a spray-bar directed at the tank wall works well. The fish tend to occupy the upper water column and surface zone.

Pseudomugilidae is the sister family to Melanotaeniidae; the forktail pairs naturally with other Pseudomugil species, small melanotaeniid rainbowfishes, micro-rasboras, and dwarf shrimp. Adult Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp are generally tolerated, though juvenile shrimp may be eaten. This species is a confirmed jumper and a covered aquarium or a tank with a tight-fitting lid is generally recommended.

Cross-reference: [threadfin-rainbowfish], [boesemani-rainbow], [neon-dwarf-rainbow] and other Pseudomugil blue-eyes.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers with no parental care; eggs are deposited singly on fine-leaved plants, spawning mops, or moss over an extended period (a few eggs per day). Adults have been reported to consume their own eggs and fry if given the opportunity, so separating eggs or using a dedicated breeding setup tends to improve yield. Eggs typically hatch in approximately 21 days; fertility has been reported to be under 50% even when eggs appear normal. Fry are large enough to accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms from the start. Spawning activity tends to increase at slightly higher temperatures (toward the upper end of the recommended range). A 1:2 or 1:3 male-to-female ratio is commonly recommended.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source for common name, tank size, water parameters, diet, temperament, grouping, tankmate recommendations, and breeding overview.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pseudomugilidae, Atheriniformes), synonyms (Popondetta furcata, Popondichthys furcatus), native range (Musa and Kwagila basins, PNG Northern and Milne Bay provinces), water parameters (temp 24–28°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 268–536 ppm), adult size (40–60 mm), tank minimum (60×30 cm base), diet, grouping (8–10 minimum recommended), breeding details (21-day incubation, <50% fertility), and sensitivity to immature tanks. Temperature upper bound averaged across AC (27°C) and SF (28°C) to yield 27°C final.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pseudomugilidae, Atheriniformes), native range (eastern PNG lowlands), adult size (males 5.0 cm, females ~4.0 cm), temperature (24–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0). Size range set to 4–5 cm reflecting the female/male dimorphism across all sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer in fine-leaved plants and spawning mops; deposits small numbers of eggs daily. Spawning is achievable but fry rearing requires micro-foods and separation from adults. Fertility tends to be low (reported under 50%).
